text: This is Logan Miller with National Surgery Review. Thank you for joining us today as we walk you through a laparoscopic appendectomy.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic appendectomy with an incision at Palmer's point in the left upper quadrant, accessing the abdomen using the OptiView technique. Two additional trochars are placed in the left lower quadrant and suprapubic position. The right lower quadrant is examined to identify the appendix, terminal ileum, and cecum. The peritoneum overlying the base of the appendix is carefully torn, followed by the use of a Maryland dissector and bowel grasper to open the base of the appendix. The appendix is then mobilized from its lateral attachments using ultrasonic shears.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to divide the mesentery of the appendix, providing hemostasis from the base to the tip. A stapler is inserted through the left lower quadrant port, positioned to take a small cecal cap with the specimen, ensuring prevention of stump appendicitis. The anatomy is confirmed before firing the stapler.

The divided appendix is placed into an endocatch bag and extracted from the abdomen. The surgeon inspects the cecum and appendiceal mesentery for hemostasis, irrigates the pelvis, and removes any fluid. Finally, all laparoscopic ports are closed.
Until next time, this is Logan Mellert with National Surgery Review.
